_source: FM 21-76 U.S. Army Survival Manual (public domain). Data compiled for GridDown offline reference.

knots.name: Bowline
knots.use: Creates a fixed loop that will not slip. Ideal rescue loop — the 'king of knots'.
knots.strength: High. Does not weaken rope significantly.
steps: Form a small loop in the standing part of the rope, leaving enough tail for your desired loop size.
Pass the working end up through the small loop from below.
Take the working end around the standing part (behind and around).
Pass the working end back down through the small loop.
Tighten by pulling standing part while holding working end and loop.
knots.source: The Ashley Book of Knots #1010. FM 21-76.

knots.name: Clove Hitch
knots.use: Quick attachment to a post or tree. Starting and finishing lashings. Good for adjustable tension.
knots.strength: Moderate. Can slip under variable load — add half hitches for security.
steps: Pass rope over post and cross over itself.
Pass rope over post again, slightly to the right of first wrap.
Tuck working end under the second wrap (the cross).
Pull both ends to tighten.
knots.warning: Can slip under alternating load. Secure with two half hitches if load direction will vary.

knots.name: Sheet Bend
knots.use: Joining two ropes of different diameter. The standard knot for joining dissimilar ropes.
knots.strength: Good for joining ropes. Stronger than square knot for different diameters.
steps: Form a bight (bend) in the thicker rope.
Pass the thinner rope up through the bight from below.
Take the thin rope around behind both ends of the thick rope's bight.
Tuck thin rope under itself where it exits the bight.
Pull both ropes to tighten.
knots.warning: Use double sheet bend for very different rope diameters — pass thin rope around twice before tucking.

knots.name: Square Knot (Reef Knot)
knots.use: Binding bandages and packages. Joining two ropes of equal diameter under non-critical loads.
knots.strength: Low for critical loads. Not suitable for joining ropes under significant tension — use sheet bend.
steps: Take two rope ends. Pass the left end over and under the right end (first half knot).
Now pass the end that is on the RIGHT over and under the end on the LEFT (second half knot).
A correct square knot: the two loops lie parallel. Left over right, then right over left.
Pull to tighten.
knots.warning: REMEMBER: Left over right, then right over left. If you do left-left or right-right you tie a granny knot which slips. Square knot is for bandages — NOT for joining ropes under load.
knots.source: Ashley Book of Knots #74. FM 21-76 for bandaging.

knots.name: Taut-Line Hitch
knots.use: Adjustable hitch for tent guy lines, tarps, lashing. Grips under load but slides when pressure released.
knots.strength: Good when loaded in correct direction.
steps: Pass rope around anchor (stake or tree).
Make two wraps around the standing part inside the loop.
Add one wrap outside and above the two wraps.
Tuck working end through to complete.
Slide knot to adjust tension — grips when pulled along standing part.
knots.warning: Only grips in one direction. Ensure load pulls knot toward anchor, not away.

knots.name: Trucker's Hitch
knots.use: Mechanical advantage 3:1 to cinch loads extremely tight. Lashing gear, securing tarps under load.
knots.strength: Extremely strong when properly tied. Creates mechanical advantage.
steps: Tie anchor point at one end of load.
In the middle of the rope, make a slipped loop (overhand loop with a bight, not the end).
Thread working end around anchor at far end of load.
Thread working end back up through the slipped loop.
Pull down on working end to tighten — 3:1 mechanical advantage.
Lock with two half hitches through the slipped loop.
knots.warning: The slipped loop is critical — it must be a loop through which you thread the rope, not a simple bend.
knots.source: Ashley Book of Knots. FM 21-76 lashing techniques.

knots.name: Prusik Hitch
knots.use: Ascending a rope. Creating a friction hitch for rope rescue and rappel backup. Load-bearing slide-and-grip.
knots.strength: Grips firmly under load. Slides when unloaded.
steps: Requires a prusik loop (short cord of smaller diameter than main rope, tied into loop with double fisherman).
Drape loop over main rope.
Pass loop around main rope and through itself two more times (total 3 wraps).
Dress wraps neatly — all wraps must lie parallel and not overlap.
Attach carabiner to loop below wraps.
To ascend: weight carabiner to grip. To slide: lift hitch with free hand to slide upward.
knots.warning: Prusik cord must be 60-75% diameter of main rope. Will not grip if diameters too similar or too different. Must dress wraps neatly.
knots.source: Karl Prusik, 1931 (public domain technique). Standard mountaineering reference.

knots.name: Figure-Eight
knots.use: Stopper knot to prevent rope running through a belay device. Also tied as a loop for anchoring.
knots.strength: Excellent. Easy to inspect and untie after loading.
steps: Make a loop in rope. Pass working end around and through the loop to make a figure-eight shape.
Pull working end to tighten into a figure-eight pattern.
For figure-eight on a bight: double the rope, then tie figure-eight with both strands.

knots.name: Double Fisherman's
knots.use: Joining two rope ends to form a prusik loop. Joining two ropes for rappelling. Extremely secure join.
knots.strength: Very high. Nearly impossible to untie after loading.
steps: Place two rope ends parallel, pointing in opposite directions.
Take one end and wrap it twice around both ropes, then pass back through both wraps.
On the other end of rope, do the same wrap in opposite direction (two wraps, thread through).
Slide both knots together by pulling the standing parts.
Finished knot: two X patterns side by side.
knots.warning: Extremely difficult to untie after loading — consider it permanent. Use overhand bend for retrievable join.

knots.name: Alpine Butterfly
knots.use: Fixed loop in middle of rope without access to ends. For isolating damaged rope section. Attaching to middle of rope.
knots.strength: Excellent in all directions, including inline pull. Can be untied after loading.
steps: Wrap rope around palm twice to make two loops.
Take upper (closer to wrist) loop and fold it over the lower loop.
Take that same loop and fold it under all the strands, then up through the middle gap.
Pull loop to tighten while dressing the knot.
Result: a fixed loop projecting from the middle of the rope.

knots.name: Round Turn and Two Half Hitches
knots.use: Secure attachment to post, ring, or tree. Resists rolling and variable load direction.
knots.strength: Good. More secure than clove hitch under variable load.
steps: Pass rope completely around the anchor twice (the 'round turn').
Take working end and make a half hitch around standing part.
Make a second half hitch below the first.
Pull to tighten.

knots.name: Bowline on a Bight
knots.use: Two fixed loops in middle of rope without access to ends. For body harness, rescue operations, rigging.
steps: Double the rope to form a bight. Work with the doubled rope as if it were a single rope.
Form a loop in the doubled rope.
Pass bight end up through the loop.
Open the bight end wide and flip it back over the entire knot.
Slide bight back up to seat under the loop.
Pull all four strands to dress and tighten.
knots.warning: Practice this before you need it. The step of opening and flipping the bight is unintuitive.
knots.source: Ashley Book of Knots #1080. FM 21-76 rescue rigging.

fire_lay_types.name: Teepee
fire_lay_types.description: Tinder ball in center. Small kindling leaned around it in cone shape. Medium fuel leaned around that. Good ignition pattern — fire naturally falls inward feeding itself.
fire_lay_types.best_for: Quick fire starting, cooking, signal fire

fire_lay_types.name: Log Cabin
fire_lay_types.description: Place two large logs parallel. Lay tinder/kindling between. Stack smaller logs perpendicular on top, building up in log-cabin pattern. Long burning.
fire_lay_types.best_for: Long burn, warmth through night, cooking platform

fire_lay_types.name: Star Fire
fire_lay_types.description: Five or more logs radiating from center like spokes of wheel. Push inward as they burn. Requires minimal wood splitting.

fire_lay_types.name: Long Fire
fire_lay_types.description: Two large logs placed parallel about shoulder width apart. Build and maintain fire between them. Logs act as cooking surface supports and wind breaks.
fire_lay_types.best_for: Cooking over fire trench, wind exposed sites

fire_lay_types.name: Dakota Hole
fire_lay_types.description: Dig two connected holes about 1 foot apart and 1 foot deep. Fire in main hole, second hole provides oxygen draft. Near-invisible fire, wind resistant.
fire_lay_types.best_for: Tactical concealment, windy conditions, fuel-efficient cooking
fire_lay_types.source: FM 21-76 Ch.7. Tom Brown Jr. Field Guide to Wilderness Survival.

signal_ground_to_air.meaning: Safe to land here (clear ground with no obstacles)
